data.title: Huntington Ingalls downgraded at Wolfe Research
data.description:
Huntington Ingalls Industries (NYSE:HII) late Wednesday was downgraded to Peer Perform from a previous investment rating of Outperform by analysts at Wolfe Research. They said the shipbuilder’s cash flow likely will be less than previously expected because of delays in completing contracts.

“We've remained constant in a view that military shipbuilding has one of the longest tails of growth; however, that growth is progressing slower (and at lower margins) due in large part to labor struggles that have led to persistent schedule slips and higher costs,” Myles Walton, analyst at Wolfe, said in an October 9 report.

Wolfe suspended its price target on Huntington (HII) with the rating downgrade.

data.title: Hedge funds set new single day record in dumping Chinese equities - Goldman Sachs
data.description:
Hedge funds recorded the largest single-day net selling of Chinese equities on Tuesday, October 8, as China's National Development and Reform Commission underwhelmed with no major stimulus, a Goldman Sachs note showed.

Hedge funds not only reduced long positions, but also increased short positions, with long sells being double the amount of short sells.

"Three-quarters of the net selling was driven by selling in A-shares as market opened up after a week long holiday. The remaining one-quarter of the selling activity was in H-shares while activity in ADRs was relatively muted." according to the research note.

"To put this in a historical context, this net selling was 1.4 times larger than the previous record for net selling and represents a 6.5 Standard Deviation event on our records." Goldman added.

After a week-long vacation in China, traders returned to a tumultuous market as the government refrained from offering the expected support to its troubled economy. This led to wild swings in share prices and record turnover on the mainland and Hong Kong bourses.

China's CSI300 blue-chip index touched its highest level in early trade on Tuesday since July 2022, while the Shanghai Composite Index also marked the highest level since December 2021. The indexes, however, gave up large gains, losing their back-from-holiday momentum.

Traders will now keep a close watch on the Finance Minister's discussion due on Saturday over moves to strengthen fiscal policy to shore up growth.

Tickers that saw notable price swings on Tuesday: Xpeng (XPEV), Li Auto (LI), NIO (NIO), Dada Nexus (DADA), Baozun (BZUN), JD.com (JD), Vipshop (VIPS), Trip.com (TCOM), Gaoto Techedu (GOTU), Dingdong (DDL), Smart Share Global (EM), QuantaSing Group (QSG), and Tuniu Corp. (TOUR).

data.title: Air Products gains after DE Shaw takes stake, pushes for board seats (update)
data.description:
Update 10:45am: Adds DE Shaw comment, confirmation, updates shares.

Air Products (NYSE:APD) rose 1.2% after hedge fund DE Shaw disclosed that it has taken a stake in the gas maker and is pushing for changes including new board directors.

DE Shaw accumulated a $1 billion economic stake in Air Products, some of which is likely through swaps, and wants three board seats, CNBC's David Faber said on Thursday, citing people familiar with the matter. DE Shaw initially sent Air Products (APD) a letter on Sept. 6.

"Given the apparent lack of urgency on the part of the Company's Board to engage in meaningful dialogue or take steps to address Air Products' persistent and long-term share price underperformance, we are today compelled to make public our analysis and recommendations for change," DE Shaw managing directors Edwin Jager and Michael O'Mary wrote in a letter on Thursday to Air Products board.

The news come after Air Products (APD) jumped 10% on Monday after a Friday report that activist investor Mantle Ridge accumulated a more than $1 billion stake in the industrial gas maker.

Mantle Ridge is said to be seeking to replace all of Air Products (APD) directors, according to Faber. Mantle Ridge met with APD on Wednesday.

Mantle Ridge, which is helmed by Paul Hilal, is expected to push the industrial gas firm on succession planning for CEO Seifi Ghasemi as well as for improvements to the company's strategy and capital allocation, the WSJ said on Friday.

DE Shaw wants Air Products (APD) to "de-risk" its large project commitments by signing offtake agreements at "reasonable return hurdles." DE Shaw, like Mantle Ridge, also wants a clear succession plan for APD CEO Ghasemi. The investor also wants APD to restructure executive compensation to improve its alignment with strategy and performance.

DE Shaw plans to nominate former Olin Corp. Chairman and CEO Scott Sutton to Air Products (APD) board, Faber also said.

More on Air Products and Chemicals

  • Air Products and Chemicals: Scoop Up This Dividend Aristocrat Now
  • Air Products and Chemicals, Inc. (APD) Jefferies Industrials Conference - (Transcript)
  • Air Products and Chemicals: Dividend Aristocrat With A Green Future
  • Air Products edges higher amid report activist Mantle Ridge will meet with gas maker
  • Air Products surges amid reported activist stake, prompts several Street upgrades

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, Dow slip as CPI rises more than expected, jobless claims comes in hot
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages slid on Thursday, as September consumer inflation comes in hotter than expected.

Early on and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.2%, the S&P 500 (SP500) was -0.2%, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.2%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was up 2 basis points to 4.09%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 4 basis points to 3.98%.

Major U.S. stock averages closed in the green on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 (SP500) and the Dow (DJI) ending at new peaks after the Federal Reserve's September meeting minutes said a "substantial majority" on its rate-setting board backed its big half-point cut to start an easing cycle.

"Markets have put in another strong performance over the last 24 hours, as optimism about the near-term economic outlook continues to build," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

"Reading the (FOMC) Minutes, CPI is important to what happens next," Economist Steven Blitz from Global Data TS Lombard said.

September Consumer Price Index +0.2% M/M vs. +0.1% expected and +0.2% prior, +2.4% Y/Y vs. +2.3% expected and +2.5% prior.

Core CPI, which excludes food and energy: +0.3% M/M vs. +0.2% expected and +0.3% prior, +3.3% Y/Y vs. +3.2% expected and +3.2% prior.

The CPI report is in particular focus after the strong jobs report last Friday, as there’s been growing speculation that the Fed might not cut at all at their next meeting in November, Allen added.

The initial jobless claims climbed more than expected in the past week.

The Fed balance sheet will come later in the day.

data.title: Top Seeking Alpha Quant picks in Financial sector with earnings set to kick off
data.description:
The S&P 500’s financials sector (NYSEARCA:XLF) has seen a robust performance in the third quarter, as it rose 10% in the period.

The heavyweight sector, which had fallen short in the second quarter, has managed to revive itself.

According to Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system, the Financials sector has an average health score of 3.58. The system awards grades based on quantitative measures, like valuation, earnings growth, and recent stock performance. The highest possible score for any individual company is 5.

Progressive (PGR), American Express (AXP), Discover Financial Services (DFS) topped the quant chart, with PGR and AXP leading the pack with a quant score of 4.94, followed by DFS at 4.92. PayPal (PYPL) takes the third sport with a quant score of 4.88.

Insurer Progressive (PGR) had beaten its Q2 EPS estimates, and reported strong data on August net written premiums.

J.P. Morgan's Jimmy Bhullar, who rates Progressive overweight, has said that the positive results were due to robust margins, low attritional losses, and favorable reserve development. In August, Goldman Sachs had also upgraded the stock to Buy from Neutral

SA analyst Patrick Kroneman, called the firm a top performer in the insurance industry as it delivered impressive returns, outperforming the S&P 500 with over 1100% growth in the last decade.

In August, American Express (AXP) was downgraded by Bank of America Securities as subdued billings volume growth and the stock's current premium valuation limit its upside potential.

“American Express has demonstrated consistent revenue growth across segments, with U.S. Consumer Services leading the way. Yet, these growth rates are normalizing,” said SA analyst, Daniel Urbina.

The company had delivered a beat to its Q2 EPS estimates, but missed on its revenue.

Discover Financial Services (DFS) had beaten both its Q2 EPS and revenue estimates.

PayPal (PYPL) had turned in stronger-than-expected Q2 earnings and revenue, boosted its 2024 earnings guidance, and raised its share repurchase outlook.

Franklin Resources (BEN) bottomed the list with a quant score of 2.17, in a quarter wherein reports of federal prosecutors investigating whether an executive at the firm's Western Asset Management unit allocated profitable trades to favored accounts came out.

Franklin Resources (BEN) was also one of the stocks expected to be impacted by the Federal Reserve’s rate cut, according to UBS.

Goldman Sachs expects XLF EPS to see a fall of 1%.

According to a FactSet report, the estimated earnings growth rate for the S&P500 is 4.2% for the third quarter, with the Energy sector expected to report the largest y-o-y earnings decline of all eleven sectors at -20.9%. Utilities sector is expected to post a 3.9% earnings growth during the quarter.

The financials sector is predicted to report a year-over-year earnings decline of -0.4% for the third quarter, according to FactSet.

FactSet added, at the industry level, banks were the only industry that is expected to report a year-over-year decline in earnings at -12%, and is also expected to be the largest contributor to the year-over-year earnings decline for the sector.

The report said, if the banks industry were excluded, the estimated earnings growth rate for the financials sector would improve to 6.9% from -0.4%.

"All told, third quarter bank results look to be a mixed bag, but seeded with multiple reasons for the market to look through near term negatives, and ahead to potentially more positive conditions (and results) in 2025," said Sean Ryan, VP/Associate Director for the banking and specialty finance sector at FactSet.

"The caveat is that the Fed’s easing, while not a large factor in 3Q results, is apt to weigh on short term NIM expectations," Ryan added.

The third quarter results of some of the companies, especially banks, will also show investors if the supersized interest rate cut by the Fed has had any impact on their earnings.

Even though the cuts took place towards the end of the quarter, traders will look to assess the impact.

SA analyst, MacroGirl had expected the rate cuts to impact financials, with mixed effects on XLF due to lower NIMs and increased loan activity.

data.title: Builders FirstSource well positioned amid new residential building recovery, Goldman says
Pfizer ex-CEO and CFO exit Starboard activist campaign (update)
data.description:
Update 10.20 AM EST: Adds comments from Starboard Value

Former Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) executives Ian Read and Frank D'Amelio have left an activist campaign against the pharmaceutical giant that hedge fund Starboard Value started despite initially pledging their support for the effort.

Read, who handpicked current Pfizer (PFE) chief Albert Bourla as his successor during his stint as CEO from 2010 to 2018, and D'Amelio, who was the company's CFO from 2007 to 2021, announced their decision late Wednesday.

"We are fully supportive of Pfizer Chairman and CEO Albert Bourla, senior management, and the board, and we are confident that over time they will deliver shareholder value," the duo said, according to a statement issued by the company's advisor, investment bank Guggenheim Securities.

Their exit from the campaign marks the latest twist in Starboard's activist drive, which reportedly launched early this month following the investor's roughly $1B stake in Pfizer (PFE) with the support of Read and D'Amelio.

In a press release on Thursday, Starboard Value alleged that people within Pfizer (PFE) and/or company representatives threatened Read and D'Amelio with possible legal action unless they publicly endorse Bourla.

The investment advisor urged the Pfizer (PFE) board to form a special committee to investigate the allegations and take appropriate action.

data.title: Osceola Gold inks multi-year agreement to expand mining operations in Nevada
data.description:
• Osceola Gold (OTCPK:OSCI) said on Thursday that it has signed a 5-year consulting agreement with the owner of mining claims in the Osceola Mining District, located in White Pine County, Nevada.
  • As per the agreement, Osceola Gold will consult on mining operations on the expansive property, and in return, receive a 30% split of the revenue generated from the mining activities.
  • The company will deploy its team to maximize the gold extraction from the Mary Ann Canyon claims. This strategic deployment aims to optimize operational efficiency and yield.
  • The agreement could enhance Osceola's financial performance, contributing to steady revenue growth and improved profit margins over the duration of the agreement.
  • The term of the agreement is five years, with automatic renewals for additional 5-year increments.
  • Shares of Osceola Gold (OTCPK:OSCI) surged over 92% on Thursday.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Lilly threatens legal action against copycat Zepbound providers: report
data.description:
Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) has reportedly sent out cease-and-desist letters to compounding pharmacies and clinics that were providing copycat versions of its hugely popular weight-loss and diabetes drugs Zepbound and Mounjaro while the medications were in short supply.

The drugmaker is demanding that compounding pharmacies, telehealth providers and medical spas stop making, selling and/or promoting knockoff versions of Zepbound and Mounjaro, which had been on the FDA’s drug shortage list since December 2022, according to Bloomberg.

The FDA allowed copycat versions of the drugs to be made and distributed while the drugs were in short supply. That changed on Oct. 2, however, when the agency took the drugs off the shortage list.

Bankers have estimated compounded versions of the drugs, which are often far cheaper than branded versions, had been generating annual sales of around $1B, according to Bloomberg.

Some compounding pharmacies are continuing to supply compounded formulations for certain patients under an FDA provision that allows for such versions when they are deemed medically necessary by a prescriber, Bloomberg said.

The Outsourcing Facilities Association, which represents bulk compounding pharmacies, has sued the FDA for taking the drugs off the shortage list, asserting that the agency was depriving patients of needed medications, Bloomberg added.

Lilly's Mounjaro and Zepbound primarily compete against Novo Nordisk's (NVO) Ozempic and Wegovy in the weight-loss/diabetes drug market.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: TLRY, GXO, TXG, and more
data.description:
Stock futures and Treasury yields dipped on Thursday after inflation data came in higher than expected, alongside a rise in unemployment. The uncertainty surrounding the Federal Reserve's future rate cuts weighed on market sentiment.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Shares of GXO Logistics (GXO) surged 6% as the company, a supply chain services provider spun off from XPO Inc. in 2021, is reportedly exploring a potential sale after receiving interest from potential buyers, according to sources familiar with the matter. The Greenwich, Connecticut-based company, with a market value of around $6B, is said to be working with financial advisers to evaluate acquisition offers, including interest from rival logistics providers. While no final decision has been made and the outcome remains uncertain, the discussions are being kept confidential.
  • CrowdStrike (CRWD) shares soared nearly 5% after being named one of RBC Capital's top North American software ideas for 2025. Despite a mid-July share price drop due to a widespread outage, the cybersecurity firm reevaluated its procedures and has rallied approximately 35% since early August. RBC analyst Matthew Hedberg believes CrowdStrike will emerge stronger from the outage, with derisked FY/25 and FY/26 estimates and a clear path to achieving $10 billion in annual recurring revenue (ARR). RBC included CrowdStrike among 11 software companies with solid growth prospects and minimal risk of 2025 estimate reductions.
  • EVgo Inc. (EVGO) shares rose over 3% after UBS upgraded the electric vehicle charging network's rating from Neutral to Buy, following the company's announcement of a $1.05B Department of Energy loan conditional commitment. UBS analyst William Grippin highlighted the increased visibility the loan provides for EVgo’s funding and the acceleration of new stall deployments beyond 2025. Grippin expects the loan to support over 1,500 new stall deployments annually over the next five years, compared to the 800-900 projected for 2024. Additionally, the decreasing likelihood of a Republican sweep in November mitigates the risks associated with EVGO's outlook. Based on these positive developments, UBS raised its price target for EVgo to $8.50 from $4.

Biggest stock losers

  • Shares of E2open Parent Holdings (ETWO), a supply chain software company, plunged 26% following the release of its mixed FQ2 results. The company's adjusted revenue fell short of analysts' expectations, primarily due to delays in finalizing some significant deals. These delays were due to extended client decision timelines. In light of these developments, the company revised its revenue forecast for FY2025 downward, projecting a range of $607M to $617M. This revised outlook is below the consensus estimate of $627.51M.
  • 10x Genomics (TXG) shares plummeted over 26% after the life-science technology company announced preliminary Q3 revenue of approximately $151.7M, a 1% decline from the same period last year and below the consensus of $162.26M. The company attributed the shortfall to modifications in its commercial processes and organization, as well as cautious consumer spending habits.
  • Tilray Brands (TLRY) shares fell as much as 6% after the company reported mixed FQ1 results with adjusted EBITDA of $9.33M, down 18% Y/Y, missing the consensus of $13.8M. Cannabis revenue dropped 13% to $61.2M, below the consensus of $74.4M, while distribution revenue dipped 1.6% to $68.1Mn, missing the $68.9M consensus. Beverage alcohol revenue surged to $56M, up from $24.2M a year ago, though it also came in below the $64.5M consensus. Wellness revenue, however, rose 11% to $14.8M, exceeding the $14.1M forecast. The company also reported a negative free cash flow of $42M, compared to negative $19.7M last year. Despite these mixed results, CEO Irwin Simon expressed optimism, citing the potential for favorable regulatory changes in the cannabis industry with both U.S. presidential candidates supporting further legalization efforts.
  • First Solar (FSLR) lost more than 7% after Jefferies lowered its expectations for the company's upcoming third-quarter results. The brokerage cited concerns over potential delays, module pricing impacts from AD/CVD determinations, and plans to sell excess Indian capacity into the U.S. Jefferies analysts anticipate a slight miss in Q3 with lower volumes compared to consensus and have reduced their volume estimates for 2025 and 2026 by 8% and 4%, respectively. Additionally, they highlighted risks of project delays into Q1, slashing their Q4 and full-year volume estimates, now 2% below consensus and at the lower end of guidance. Jefferies also expected only modest backlog additions of 2GW in Q3, compared to the 3.2GW consensus, with momentum likely resuming in early 2025.
